evidence_quotes.quote: video games (including offline video games) are switched off by publishers.

paragraphs.text: Ladies and gentlemen, I would like to ask you to ensure the futility of video games sold or licensed to consumers in the EU. The current situation is that video games (including offline video games) are switched off by publishers. From today to tomorrow, games are rendered incapacitated and players lose the opportunity to play them, even in a single player mode. Hundreds of games have already happened and hundreds of games risk being switched off. You can imagine today’s practice of publishers as follows: You have settlers of Catan on the shelves. The current editor of Catan settlers is now launching Catan 2 settlers. The next day, the editor is over and says you can buy Siedler 2 if you want, but the box with you on the shelves, which you can no longer play. Your old box will then be removed from you and you will not be able to appeal. For example, we want to make sure you have fun with Catan settlers. And we can no longer do this with the old part. However, this is something that is frustrated. Blizzard Entertainment Bringt Overwatch 2 and switches off Overwatch 1. However, many people were fully satisfied with the first part, found it better, their communities built up in this game. Everything for the publisher, and finally (here my assumption) the new game must be played. And if you don’t stop from the old game, we force you to do so. Diablo 2 Ressurrected (also of Blizzard) is a legendary game that is exposed to a similar risk. The current diablo 4 is unpopular and is perceived as poorly designed. However, Diablo 2 (Blizzard’s old game, which everyone has already bought) is in competition with this. In addition to the alleged wrong, current practices of publishers pose a threat to the quality and safety of future video games. According to today’s motto, publishers can destroy alternatives to their products so that their new products are allegedly better represented. It is irrelevant here if the new product is of poor quality or is loaded with gambling mechanisms and microtransactions. It may be added that the current publishers are not the original developers (or original publishers) of off-plays. They can therefore destroy old games in order to favour their own developments in competition on the market. I call on you – Please make sure that the Digital Fairness Act obliges publishers to leave video games in a fuseable (playable) state, even if no further resources are invested in the games by the publishers. Yours sincerely,
